sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian horticultural research, the rare-fruit and horticultural society archives, and state agriculture and biosecurity sources.

core.intro: Across Australia the one thing every blueberry must have is the same: a strongly acidic soil. Get the soil right and match the type to your winter, and blueberries are productive, long-lived shrubs. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; these notes apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Blueberries fall into three groups, and picking the one that fits your winter is the first decision. Northern highbush (such as Brigitta, Denise and Bluecrop) are deciduous, need a genuinely cold winter of roughly 750 to 1000 chill hours, and suit cool districts. Southern highbush (such as Biloxi, Misty, Sharpblue and Gulf Coast) are low-chill at about 250 to 600 hours, stay semi-evergreen or evergreen in warm areas, and are the type that does best across most of Australia. Rabbiteye (such as Powder Blue, Brightwell and Climax) are vigorous, heat and drought hardy and late-ripening, and want about 450 to 600 hours. Decide which group matches your climate first, then choose a named variety. The in-stock table above leans heavily to low-chill southern highbush and rabbiteye, which is what suits most of the country.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two bushes?
sections.body: It depends on the type. Highbush blueberries, both northern and southern, are self-fertile, so a single bush will crop, but they set more and bigger berries when a second variety of the same type flowers alongside. Rabbiteye are different: they are self-incompatible and must have a second, different rabbiteye variety nearby to fruit at all, which is why nurseries sell them in matched pairs (Brightwell with Premier, Powder Blue with Tifblue). The rule of thumb is to plant two different varieties of the same group, not a highbush next to a rabbiteye, because the two flower at different times. Bees do the work, so avoid spraying while the bushes are in flower.

sections.heading: The acid soil rule
sections.body: This is the step that makes or breaks a blueberry. They are ericaceous plants (the azalea and rhododendron family) with fine, shallow roots and no root hairs, and they thrive only in a strongly acidic soil of about pH 4.5 to 5.5, with the highbush types liking it down nearer 4.5. Most Australian soils are too alkaline for them, so test the pH first. If it reads above about 5.5, dig in elemental sulphur six to twelve months ahead and re-test before planting. Work in plenty of acidic organic matter such as aged pine bark, peat or composted softwood sawdust, and keep manures, mushroom compost and lime well away, because they push the pH the wrong way. If your ground is naturally alkaline or limey, the simplest answer is to grow in raised beds or large pots of an ericaceous (azalea and camellia) mix rather than fight the native soil. Mulch with a coarse acidic mulch such as pine bark or woodchip, kept clear of the stems (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Those shallow roots dry out and run short of food faster than most fruit plants, so blueberries want steady moisture right through the season, ideally from drip or a soaker hose rather than overhead watering, which spreads leaf disease. They are also sensitive to salt, so where town or bore water is hard or salty, rainwater gives far better results. Feed lightly, because blueberries are easily over-fed and too much nitrogen cuts cropping: use an acidic or ericaceous fertiliser at about half the rate you would give other plants, choose ammonium-based nitrogen (sulphate of ammonia) rather than nitrate, go easy on phosphorus, and never lime. If a soil test shows you need calcium, add it as gypsum, which does not raise the pH.

sections.heading: Pruning
sections.body: For the first year or two, rub off the flowers so the plant builds a strong framework instead of fruiting itself into a weak, twiggy bush. From about the third year, prune in late winter: cut out the oldest, twiggiest canes (around a quarter of the bush) right at the base each year to drive strong new wood, which carries the best fruit. Evergreen southern highbush in warm districts can instead be tidied and tip-pruned after harvest, which helps time the next flush of growth and flower.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Blueberries sweeten on the bush and barely improve once picked, so patience pays off. The berries turn blue about a week before they are fully ripe, so leave them a few more days and pick only the ones that drop into your hand with a gentle roll of the thumb, going back over each bush every seven to ten days. A young bush gives a handful in its second year and a real crop from the third or fourth, and a well-kept plant stays productive for a decade or more, with rabbiteye and northern highbush the longest-lived. Net the bushes before the fruit colours, because birds are the number one blueberry pest.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named potted variety chosen for your climate, and check the label for which of the three groups it belongs to (southern highbush, northern highbush or rabbiteye) and its chill needs, since that decides whether it will crop where you live. Remember that rabbiteye need a second rabbiteye as a partner, so buy them two at a time. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow blueberries in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and a pot is often the best way to grow them, because you control the soil completely. Fill a large container (at least 40 cm across) with an ericaceous or azalea and camellia mix, water often since pots dry out fast, feed lightly, and stand it in full sun. A potted bush is also easy to net against birds and easy to move out of extreme heat.

faqs.q: How long until a blueberry bush fruits, and how long does it live?
faqs.a: Expect a few berries in the second year, a worthwhile crop from the third or fourth, and full bearing by about year five or six. A healthy, well-fed bush in the right soil keeps cropping for ten to twenty years or more, with rabbiteye and northern highbush the longest-lived of the three types.

faqs.q: Why are my blueberry leaves yellow between green veins?
faqs.a: Yellow leaves with green veins are iron chlorosis, and on a blueberry that almost always means the soil is not acidic enough for the roots to take up iron. Test the pH, and if it is above about 5.5, work in more elemental sulphur and switch to an acidic fertiliser. (Whole leaves turning red in autumn on a deciduous variety is just normal seasonal colour.)

faqs.q: Do blueberry bushes lose their leaves in winter?
faqs.a: It depends on the type. Northern highbush are deciduous, so they drop their leaves and colour up in autumn, while southern highbush and rabbiteye usually stay semi-evergreen or fully evergreen in warm districts and only partly shed their leaves where winters are cold.

WA.intro: Growing blueberries in Western Australia. The south-west can grow excellent blueberries, but WA's alkaline sands and hard water make the acid-soil rule the real job, so most growers raise them in beds or pots of acidic mix rather than in the native ground.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Western Australia's south-west grows good blueberries. The warm Perth plain has mild winters that suit low-chill southern highbush and rabbiteye, while the cooler Great Southern and the karri country near Manjimup and Pemberton pick up enough chill for the deciduous northern highbush as well. The Mediterranean pattern of dry summers and low humidity is a genuine advantage, because it keeps the leaf diseases that plague the humid east coast to a minimum. The harder part in WA is the soil, since the native sand is alkaline and the bore water is often hard or salty, both of which blueberries dislike.

sections.heading: Where blueberries grow in WA
sections.body: Plantings cluster in the cooler, wetter parts of the south-west: the Perth Hills around Bickley, the Donnybrook district, the karri belt near Manjimup, and the Great Southern around Albany and Mount Barker. Warm Perth gardens grow the low-chill types well too, as long as they are given a raised bed or pots of acidic mix.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: Low-chill southern highbush in the warm Perth districts can start ripening in late spring, from about October, while the cooler south-west and Great Southern carry on through summer, roughly December to February.

sections.heading: Pests, and blueberry rust in WA
sections.body: Birds are the main pest, so net early. WA's dry summers spare blueberries most of the leaf diseases that trouble the eastern states, and Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established across the south-west whereas Queensland fruit fly is not, is only a minor risk on ripe berries. Blueberry rust, a wind-spread fungal disease caused by Thekopsora minima, was long kept out of WA but reached the state in 2022, which is one more reason to buy clean, locally grown plants.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from interstate must meet import conditions, and many eastern nurseries will not post to WA at all, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here. Buying WA-grown stock also lowers the risk of bringing blueberry rust into your garden.

faqs.a: On the warm coastal plain, stick to low-chill southern highbush such as Biloxi, Misty and Sharpblue, or rabbiteye such as Powder Blue planted with a partner. Save the deciduous northern highbush for the colder hills and the Great Southern, where winters are long and cold enough to satisfy them.

faqs.q: Can blueberries cope with Perth's sandy, alkaline soil and bore water?
faqs.a: Not in the ground as it is. The native sand is too alkaline and the bore water is often too hard or salty for blueberries, so grow them in a raised bed or large pots of an acidic ericaceous mix, mulch heavily, and water with rainwater or low-salt water wherever you can.

QLD.intro: Growing blueberries in Queensland. Blueberries are not the tropical fruit they might seem, but low-chill, evergreen types crop well across the warm subtropics as long as you build them a properly acidic bed.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's mild winters rule out the cold-loving northern highbush, so the field here is low-chill southern highbush and rabbiteye, which are bred for exactly these conditions. In the warm subtropics the bushes hold their leaves through winter and crop earlier and over a longer season than they do down south, and a vigorous evergreen bush will sometimes give a second, lighter flush. The trade-off is humidity, which lifts fungal disease pressure, so give bushes full sun, room and airflow.

sections.heading: Where blueberries grow in QLD
sections.body: The biggest plantings are around Bundaberg, with more through the Sunshine Coast hinterland and on the cooler, higher Granite Belt and Atherton Tablelands, which pick up extra chill. Backyard bushes do well right across the subtropical south-east in a well-made acidic bed.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland opens the national blueberry season. Evergreen southern highbush on the warm coast can be picking from late autumn and through winter into spring, roughly May to October, well ahead of the southern states.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic and will sting ripening blueberries, so monitor and bait for it. The humid, wet summers also favour blueberry rust (Thekopsora minima), which has been present in Queensland for years, along with other leaf spots, so choose open, airy positions and water at the roots rather than over the leaves. Birds, as everywhere, are the biggest single threat to the crop.

faqs.q: Can you grow blueberries in Brisbane and the subtropics?
faqs.a: Yes. Brisbane and the warm coast grow low-chill evergreen blueberries well, the main jobs being to build a properly acidic bed or pot and to keep the bushes open and netted. Just skip the northern highbush, which never gets the cold winter it needs this far north.

No, which surprises people. Despite the warm-climate types sold up north, blueberries are temperate shrubs in the same family as azaleas, and even the low-chill kinds want a cool winter and a strongly acidic soil rather than tropical heat and humidity all year round.

NSW.intro: Growing blueberries in New South Wales. NSW grows more blueberries than any other state, and you can match a type to almost any part of it, from the warm north coast to the cold tablelands.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: New South Wales spans the full blueberry range. The warm, humid north coast suits evergreen southern highbush and rabbiteye, grown under nets and tunnels on a large commercial scale, while the cold New England and Northern Tablelands have the winter chill for the deciduous northern highbush. The main climate watch-out on the coast is humidity, which favours leaf disease, balanced by a long, mild growing season.

sections.heading: Where blueberries grow in NSW
sections.body: The mid-north coast around Coffs Harbour, Corindi, Woolgoolga and Sandy Beach is the heart of the Australian industry and grows the large majority of the national crop. Cooler inland pockets such as the Dorrigo plateau and the Northern Tablelands carry the deciduous types instead.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the Coffs Harbour coast the evergreen crop is picked across a remarkably long season, from about June right through to February, peaking in the warmer months. The cool tablelands ripen their deciduous bushes later in summer, around December to February.

sections.heading: Pests, and blueberry rust in NSW
sections.body: The north coast is the Australian stronghold of blueberry rust (Thekopsora minima), a wind-spread fungal disease of the leaves and fruit that has been established here for years, so airflow, resistant varieties and clean stock all matter. Queensland fruit fly is a host risk on the coast and inland, and birds are a constant pressure, which is why the commercial farms are so heavily netted.

faqs.q: Why are there so many netted tunnels around Coffs Harbour?
faqs.a: Those are protected-cropping blueberry farms. The nets and polytunnels keep birds and hail off, hold weather and disease damage down, and let growers pick clean fruit over a long season, which is how the Coffs coast supplies most of Australia's blueberries.

faqs.q: Which blueberries suit the cold NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: The deciduous northern highbush, the type warmer states cannot satisfy, come into their own here. The cold New England and Southern Tablelands give enough winter chill for varieties like Brigitta and Denise, which ripen a little later and store well. Treat them to the same acidic soil and bird netting as anywhere else.

VIC.intro: Growing blueberries in Victoria. Victoria's reliably cold winters let it grow the full range of blueberries, including the deciduous northern highbush that warmer states struggle to satisfy.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria gives blueberries the cold winter many of them want. Its dependable chill suits the deciduous northern highbush, prized for large, firm berries and long life, and the milder districts grow southern highbush and rabbiteye just as happily. The cool season ripens the fruit later and larger, and the lower summer humidity inland keeps leaf disease down, although tender young spring growth can be caught by a late frost.

sections.heading: Where blueberries grow in VIC
sections.body: The Yarra Valley and the hills around Silvan and Wandin are the traditional Victorian blueberry district, with more plantings through Gippsland, the Dandenong Ranges and the Macedon Ranges. Most of the state is cool enough to grow the deciduous types well.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria picks late. Its cool-climate bushes ripen mainly from December through to March, and in the coldest districts into April, well after the northern crops have finished for the year.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Birds are the main pest, so net before the fruit colours. Queensland fruit fly has spread into parts of northern Victoria and can sting ripening berries, and blueberry rust (Thekopsora minima) reached the state in recent years, though the cooler, drier inland keeps fungal pressure lower than on the humid NSW coast.

sections.heading: Frost on young growth
sections.body: Established blueberries are cold-hardy, but a late spring frost can damage open blossom, especially on early-flowering evergreen types. In frost-prone valleys, lean on the later-flowering northern highbush, plant where cold air can drain away downhill, and be ready to cover bushes on the coldest spring nights.

faqs.q: Which blueberries suit Melbourne and cool Victoria?
faqs.a: Victoria is one of the few parts of Australia that grows the classic deciduous northern highbush well, so varieties like Brigitta, Denise and Bluecrop are a great choice, alongside a southern highbush such as Misty for an earlier pick. The reliable winter cold is the advantage here, not the problem.

faqs.q: Do blueberries need full sun in Victoria's cooler climate?
faqs.a: Yes. Even in the cool south, blueberries crop best in full sun, which ripens the fruit and keeps the bush airy and less disease-prone. A little afternoon shade only helps in the hottest inland spots, not in and around Melbourne.
